cards.hook: Whose opinion decides if a game is actually good?
cards.caption: Whose review actually tells you a game is good? Critics, streamers, or the people playing it? Weak hook, but the debate my feed keeps surfacing.
cards.stance: Player reviews beat critic scores every single time.
triggers: off-target: meta discussion, not a release

cards.hook: Every indie game shipping this week, in one grid.
cards.caption: Every indie release this week, ranked in one grid. Cat Mail, Mesmalic, and a dozen more you won't see on the big storefronts. The launches my feed catches before Steam does.
cards.stance: The best games this year won't come from a studio you've heard of.

cards.hook: See exactly how Unity renders every frame.
cards.caption: Unity's Frame Debugger shows you how each frame is built, step by step. Handy for devs, but a tutorial, not a release. Filed under gamedev tips.
triggers: off-target: dev tutorial, not a new release

cards.hook: A solo dev spent a week just cleaning up code.
cards.caption: Not every gamedev week is new features. This one was refactoring Chrono Crank behind the scenes. Honest devlog, but low signal for a releases feed.

cards.hook: Help me pick a game idea to build.
cards.caption: A dev asking the timeline to help choose which game concept to build first. A prompt, not a release. Off-target for this feed.

cards.hook: Resetting your Xbox, explained.
cards.caption: A corporate Xbox news link dressed up with gamedev hashtags. No game, no release, no hook. Reject for a releases feed.

cards.hook: A retro horror demo where a detective hunts a shifting nightmare.
cards.caption: Scarlet Lake: a retro survival-horror demo where detective Ben Murphy digs into a small town that keeps mutating around him. Free demo out now. The launches my feed finds first.
cards.stance: Pixel-art horror scares harder than any photoreal AAA engine.

cards.hook: AAA, AA, indie — the labels don't matter anymore.
cards.caption: So many games ship now that the AAA vs indie line is meaningless. An enjoyable game is an enjoyable game. A decent take, but a reply, not a release.
cards.stance: The AAA-vs-indie war is a marketing invention players stopped caring about.

cards.hook: The Dreamcast still gets new games in 2026
cards.caption: The Dreamcast still gets new physical indie releases every year — 25 years after it died. No AAA studio can kill a scene that loves the hardware. The niche gems my feed keeps.
cards.stance: Dead consoles have healthier release schedules than half of AAA.

cards.hook: Sells good? Studio gone. Sells bad? Studio gone.
cards.caption: Game sells good — studio gone. Sells bad — studio gone. Dev hell — studio gone. The layoffs aren't about your game at all. The takes my feed keeps before the timeline buries them.
cards.stance: The games industry needs a hard reset — the studio dies no matter what you ship.

cards.hook: 96% of indie games make no money
cards.caption: 96% of indie games make almost nothing — including ones you loved. The dream is real, the odds are brutal. The hard numbers my feed surfaces before you sink 3 years in.
cards.stance: Passion isn't a business model — most indie devs learn that too late.

cards.hook: PlayStation just killed physical indie releases
cards.caption: A dev who shipped a physical indie game calls it one of PlayStation's worst decisions ever. Collectors and players still want discs. The industry takes my feed keeps for you.
cards.stance: Killing physical media is killing the games that outlive their servers.

cards.hook: Should you actually make a video game?
cards.caption: Real talk from devs behind Quartet, Geo Mythica and Shrine's Legacy on whether you should build a game at all. Solid, but it's a soft interview clip — weak hook for a cold feed.

cards.hook: The game dev advice you actually need
cards.caption: Sponsored game-dev advice clip — decent creator, but it's a plug with a soft hook and no release. Doesn't carry as new-releases proof.

cards.hook: 1 year of game dev, zero prior experience
cards.caption: A full year of solo game dev with no prior experience, compressed to 30 seconds — built entirely in Godot. Proof the barrier dropped. The maker stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: You don't need a studio or a degree — you need Godot and a year.

cards.hook: No game over sixty quid is more than a 7/10
cards.caption: Hot take: no game that costs over sixty quid is more than a 7/10. The reviews-as-art vs product debate, flipped. The gaming takes my feed keeps before the timeline.
cards.stance: $70 pricing capped how good games are allowed to be — and reviewers stopped noticing.

cards.hook: Indie devs don't owe you a $100 deluxe edition.
cards.caption: Indie devs don't owe you a $100 deluxe edition. Build a kick-ass product and players show up. The take my feed kept out of 5,000 posts about game launches.
cards.stance: The best indie games ship without a collector's edition, not despite it.

cards.hook: Steam and GoG killed the physical game disc.
cards.caption: Steam and GoG made libraries so trustworthy nobody misses discs anymore. Most indie devs won't press one again. The debate my feed pulled from the launch-day noise.
cards.stance: Physical PC releases are dead and nobody actually cares.

cards.hook: A fresh batch of indie games dropped on Steam today.
cards.caption: A fresh batch of indie games dropped on Steam and Switch today. The small launches nobody tweets about are usually the good ones. Found before the timeline buried them.

cards.hook: An atmospheric driving game through Dutch polders and windmills.
cards.caption: An atmospheric driving game through Dutch dikes, polders and windmills, built in public in Unity. The weird gem my feed keeps while the timeline chases AAA trailers.

cards.hook: 99k views, 10 years of games, all free by donation.
cards.caption: 99k views. 10+ years shipping games. Every one free, funded entirely by voluntary donations. Proof indie doesn't need a price tag. The story my feed kept for you.
cards.stance: Free-by-donation is a more durable model than most indie devs admit.

cards.hook: Reusing game assets isn't lazy — it's how games ship
cards.caption: Reusing game assets isn't lazy — it's how good games ship on time. The 'everything from scratch' myth kills more indies than bad ideas do. Hot takes my feed keeps.
cards.stance: Asset reuse is craft, not corner-cutting — the best studios do it constantly.

cards.hook: Enemy AI got dumber as graphics got prettier
cards.caption: Enemy AI got dumber as graphics got prettier — and we traded brilliant behavior for realistic reflections. Remember when enemies flanked you? The gamedev takes my feed keeps.
cards.stance: We spent a decade on lighting and let enemy AI rot — realism was a bad trade.

cards.hook: New indie game 'Charmed Journey' hits the 2026 lineup
cards.caption: Indie roadmap drop: Charmed Journey lands in the July-Oct 2026 lineup. Small-studio release calendars are where the good stuff hides. Found before the timeline.

cards.hook: The game that made you a dev is already unplayable
cards.caption: The game that made you fall in love with games is probably already gone — pulled, delisted, unplayable 12 years later. Digital gaming forgets fast. The stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: Delisting is quiet cultural erasure — we're losing the games that made us.

cards.hook: Why 'genre' is quietly killing game design
cards.caption: A designer argues 'genre' is a trap that flattens what games could be. Worth the watch if you build them. Design deep-dives my feed keeps.

cards.hook: Is shipping a game just endless bug-fixing?
cards.caption: Real question for indie devs: is a release just 'bug-fixing is a nightmare' — or is there more happening behind the scenes? Reply-bait my feed keeps.

cards.hook: The beginner's guide to shipping your first game
cards.caption: A no-fluff beginner's path into indie game dev — from 'how do I start' to a playable release. For anyone who's been meaning to build one. The stuff my feed keeps.
